planting.note: Direct sow in early spring on a firm seedbed once soil temperatures reach 65°F; press seed into the soil surface without deep coverage, as big bluestem needs warmth and light contact to germinate well.

directions: Broadcast seed onto a weed-suppressed, firm seedbed in full sun after the last frost, pressing seed lightly into the surface and keeping the area moist for the first three to four weeks until seedlings emerge. Plants establish slowly in the first season, sending energy into deep roots rather than top growth, so avoid mowing below six inches and withhold fertilizer entirely. By the second and third year, clumps reach their full height of four to eight feet, bearing the distinctive three-branched turkey-foot seed head in August and September. Cut plants back to four to six inches in late winter before new growth begins, and expect spectacular copper-bronze fall color each autumn.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring into saline or alkali soils, spacing plugs 12–18 inches apart; plants spread vigorously by rhizome once roots are established and require no soil amendment.

directions: Set divisions into saline, alkali, or seasonally flooded soil in full sun during spring, pressing roots firmly into the planting site and watering in well to eliminate air pockets. Salt grass is one of the few natives that thrives in coastal salt marshes and alkali flats where soil salinity would kill most other plants, making it an excellent choice for erosion control along tidal edges and dune margins. Plants spread steadily by rhizome to form a dense, low mat that suppresses weeds and stabilizes disturbed soils without any supplemental fertilizer or irrigation once established. Allow the planting to spread naturally, and mow or cut back only if needed to control boundaries.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring after the last frost into moist, well-drained soil; in zone 8, mulch the root zone heavily in winter as tops may die back but roots typically survive.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, organically rich soil in full sun to partial shade, setting the crown at or just below the soil surface and watering deeply to establish. Florida gama grass is a compact relative of eastern gamagrass suited to the understory and margins of Florida hammocks, flatwoods, and wet prairie edges, and it performs well in garden beds with consistent moisture. Apply a two- to three-inch layer of mulch around the base to conserve soil moisture and protect the crown in the cooler end of its range. Divide clumps every three to four years in spring to maintain vigor and expand the planting.

planting.note: Transplant divisions or plugs in spring into loose, sandy, well-drained soil directly on dunes or coastal berms; no soil amendment is needed and plants establish quickly in infertile sand.

directions: Set divisions into dry, infertile coastal sand in full sun in spring, spacing plants 24–36 inches apart and watering once to settle roots; after the first few weeks, supplemental irrigation is rarely needed. Bitter panicgrass is one of the most effective native dune stabilizers along the Atlantic and Gulf Coasts, anchoring shifting sand with deep roots while its blue-green foliage tolerates salt spray, wind, and extended drought with ease. Plants spread slowly by rhizome, filling gaps between transplants within two to three seasons without becoming invasive. Avoid fertilizing, as lean sandy soil produces the strongest, most wind-resistant growth.

planting.note: Transplant divisions in spring into consistently moist or wet soil in partial to full shade; plants adapt well to swamp margins, rain gardens, and shaded stream banks where standing water is intermittent.

directions: Plant divisions in spring into moist to wet, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade, setting crowns at soil level and watering thoroughly to establish firm root contact. Southern waxy sedge is native to swamp margins and moist woodland understories throughout the southeastern United States, where its glaucous blue-green leaves provide year-round color in spots too wet and shaded for most ornamental grasses. Keep the root zone consistently moist, especially during the first growing season, and apply a light layer of leaf mulch to replicate the forest-floor conditions this sedge prefers. Divide clumps every three to four years in spring to refresh vigor and propagate new plants for expanding a shaded rain garden or streamside planting.
